coredata.dc:title: Mitigation of chemical degradation in perfluorosulfonic acid proton exchange membrane using regenerable hindered amine functionalized carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Henghui
Xu, Shaoyi
Zhou, Jiexin
Luo, Feiyan
Fan, Jiantao
Li, Hui
coredata.dc:description: The chemical stability of perfluorosulfonic acid (PFSA) has a significant impact on the lifetime of a proton exchange membrane (PEM). Herein, we describe the design and fabrication of a novel PEM containing hindered amine functionalized carbon quantum dots (HA-CQDs) to improve the PEM's chemical stability. The novel CQDs are prepared by a hydrothermal reaction using citric acid as the carbon source, while groups of the free radical scavenger 4-amino-2, 2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine are used to modify the oxygen-containing groups on the CQDs' surface. Benefiting from the introduction of the radical-scavenging groups, the HA-CQDs effectively eliminate active free radicals, thereby improving the PEM's chemical stability. Mass loss, FTIR, SEM, and SAXS are used to evaluate the PEM's chemical stability, surface morphology, and proton conduction by side-chain functional groups via free radicals. The electrochemical performance of the oxidized PEM is evaluated from proton conductivity and single-cell performance, and the free radical scavenging capability is characterized using the 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl radical (DPPH) radical method. Our work reveals that introducing hindered amines can eliminate the free radicals generated by electrochemical reactions and effectively mitigate the chemical degradation of a PEM. This paper provides a promising way to improve the chemical stability of PFSA and enhance the lifetime of PEMs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Proton exchange membrane
Chemical stability
Radical scavenger groups
Hindered amine
Single-cell performance

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Properties of the HA-CQDs and HA-CQDs/PFSA composite membranes
Preparation and characterization of CQDs and HA-CQDs
Performance and characterization of HA-CQDs/PFSA composite membrane
Evaluation of chemical stability for PEM
Impact of chemical degradation on structure and morphology
Effect of chemical degradation on PEM
Free radical scavenging ability of composite membranes

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Shenzhen Science, Technology Projects for Sustainable Development

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Southern University of Science and Technology

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Key Research and Development Program of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Key Research and Development Program of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: When the membranes were treated with Fenton's reagent, their chemical structures were attacked by free radicals, and the number of side-chain functional groups correspondingly decreased [23]. FTIR was used to characterize changes in the chemical structures of the various PEMs, which were immersed in Fenton's reagent for 0, 1, 3, or 5 days. As shown in Fig. 5(a?d), the peak around 1423 cm?1 is assignable to C-S bonds, the peak around 1070 cm?1 is assignable to S-OH groups, and the peak around 969 cm?1 is assignable to C-F bonds [24]. It was observed that the intensity ratios (S-OH to C-F) of the PFSA and CQDs/PFSA membranes at 1070 cm?1 and 969 cm?1 were significantly reduced when the membranes were immersed in Fenton's reagent, indicating that part of the -SO3H groups within the membrane had been degraded and washed away. However, the intensity ratios of the HA-CQDs/PFSA and Gore 740 membranes declined only slightly, demonstrating that fewer -SO3H groups were degraded under identical treatment conditions. The IEC values of the membranes treated with Fenton's reagent for different times further supported this conclusion: compared with raw PFSA and CQDs/PFSA membranes, the IEC value of the HA-CQDs/PFSA membrane dropped only slightly after treatment for 5 days. It is well known that -SO3H groups work as proton transfer carriers in PFSA-based PEMs; hence, the proton conductivity and cell performance of membranes drop when the number of -SO3H groups decreases. These results show that the introduction of HA free radical scavengers protected the chemical structure of the PFSA from free radical damage, and the HA-CQDs/PFSA composite membrane thus exhibited high chemical stability.This work was financially supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2020YFB1505500 and 2018YFB1502503), the Guangdong Key R&D Program (2020B0909040001), the Shenzhen Science, Technology Projects for Sustainable Development (KCXFZ202002011010317), and Shenzhen Free Exploration (JCYJ20190809163611271).

This work was financially supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China ( 2020YFB1505500 and 2018YFB1502503 ), the Guangdong Key R&D Program ( 2020B0909040001 ), the Shenzhen Science, Technology Projects for Sustainable Development ( KCXFZ202002011010317 ), and Shenzhen Free Exploration ( JCYJ20190809163611271 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis of CQDs and HA-CQDs, and the scavenging of free radicals by HA (Denisov cycle).
(a, b) Effective diameter and polydispersity of CQDs and HA-CQDs, measured by DLS; (c, d) zeta potential of CQDs and HA-CQDs dispersed in water–ethanol solution; (e) TEM images of HA-CQDs.
The proton conductivity of the PFSA, Gore 740, and HA-CQDs/PFSA composite membranes with various doping dosages under different RHs.
(a) Mass loss in the various membranes after 1–5 days. (b) The relationship between HA-CQDs doping dosage and oxidation stability.

FTIR of the membranes treated with Fenton's reagent for various times: (a) PFSA, (b) Gore 740, (c) CQDs/PFSA, and (d) HA-CQDs/PFSA. IEC of the membranes: (e) PFSA, (f) Gore 740, (g) CQDs/PFSA, and (h) HA-CQDs/PFSA.
SAXS profiles of PFSA, Gore 740, CQDs/PFSA, and HA-CQDs/PFSA composite membranes (a) before and (b) after treatment with Fenton's reagent for 5 days.
Proton conductivity of membranes with different degradation levels (test conditions: 80 °C and 50% RH).
Single-cell performance of MEAs based on (a) PFSA, (b) Gore 740, (c) CQDs/PFSA, and (d) HA-CQDs/PFSA membranes, treated for various times (test conditions: 80 °C and 50% RH).

(a) UV–vis absorption curve of DPPH·; (b) absorbance of DPPH· solution containing PFSA, Gore 740, CQDs/PFSA, or HA-CQDs/PFSA composite membranes at various test times; (c) DPPH· radical scavenging rate of various membranes for different immersion times.
The ion exchange capacity, water uptake, and swelling ratio of the membranes.

head.ce:title: Mitigation of chemical degradation in perfluorosulfonic acid proton exchange membrane using regenerable hindered amine functionalized carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering
Southern University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The chemical stability of perfluorosulfonic acid (PFSA) has a significant impact on the lifetime of a proton exchange membrane (PEM). Herein, we describe the design and fabrication of a novel PEM containing hindered amine functionalized carbon quantum dots (HA-CQDs) to improve the PEM's chemical stability. The novel CQDs are prepared by a hydrothermal reaction using citric acid as the carbon source, while groups of the free radical scavenger 4-amino-2, 2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine are used to modify the oxygen-containing groups on the CQDs' surface. Benefiting from the introduction of the radical-scavenging groups, the HA-CQDs effectively eliminate active free radicals, thereby improving the PEM's chemical stability. Mass loss, FTIR, SEM, and SAXS are used to evaluate the PEM's chemical stability, surface morphology, and proton conduction by side-chain functional groups via free radicals. The electrochemical performance of the oxidized PEM is evaluated from proton conductivity and single-cell performance, and the free radical scavenging capability is characterized using the 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl radical (DPPH) radical method. Our work reveals that introducing hindered amines can eliminate the free radicals generated by electrochemical reactions and effectively mitigate the chemical degradation of a PEM. This paper provides a promising way to improve the chemical stability of PFSA and enhance the lifetime of PEMs.

Introduction of the radical scavenger groups, the HA-CQDs/PFSA membrane effectively eliminate active free radicals, thereby improving the PEM's chemical stability.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Among the numerous ionomers currently available, perfluorosulfonic acids (PFSAs) are the most promising materials for the proton exchange membrane (PEM) in fuel cells, due to their remarkable thermal stability, high ionic conductivity, and excellent electrochemical performance [1].

However, the performance of PFSA-based PEMs slowly degrades during operation as the membrane's structure is destroyed by free radicals, heat, and machinery stress [2]. One of the major causes of PEM degradation is the destruction of its chemical structure [3], which comes under attack from highly reactive radicals — H·, HO·, and HOO· — initiated by their in situ generation during the electrochemical reactions of an operating fuel cell [4]. Such highly reactive radicals are generated at the cathode side by way of an incomplete oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) due to hydrogen or oxygen permeation through the PEM. These free radicals attack the side-chain functional groups and main-chain C-F bonds of the PFSA polymer, decomposing the PEM through chain depolymerization accompanied by increasing H2 crossover and decreasing open-circuit voltage [5]; the membrane electrode assembly's performance worsens, ultimately resulting in cell failure.

It has been previously reported that the incorporation of free radical scavengers or quenchers can mitigate radical attacks on a membrane [6]. For instance, transition metal cations (such as Ce3+ and Mn2+) and their oxides (including CeO2, MnO2, and ZrO2) have been introduced into PEMs to improve the chemical stability of PFSA polymers [7]. However, these membranes exhibited poor proton conductivity and cell performance because the H+ of the -SO3H group in the PFSA were exchanged for transition metal cations, decreasing the number of proton hopping sites and blocking transport pathways during cell operation [8]. More significantly, X-ray fluorescence analysis has shown that a portion of cations migrate from inside the PEM to outside the MEA; the free radical scavenging ability gradually decreases as the cation concentration drops, inevitably shortening the fuel cell's lifetime [9]. Using CeO2 or MnO2 significantly enhances fuel cell stability, but their large particle size (>100 nm) and low dispersibility in the PFSA ionomer matrix lead to uneven distribution of the radical scavengers in the PEM. Furthermore, CeO2 may reduce the crystallinity of PFSA, block the proton transport channels, and reduce the proton conduction sites, which can significantly reduce the membrane's dimensional stability and proton conductivity [10]. Vinothkannan et al. prepared a material composed of cerium oxide-anchored amine-functionalized carbon nanotubes (CeO2-ACNTs) and used this as a radical scavenger and solid proton conductor for Nafion PEMs. The existence of ACNTs in this membrane offered proton conductivity, and the addition of CeO2 mitigated against chemical degradation of the membrane [11].

In addition to providing proton conductivity, carbon nanomaterials — carbon nanotubes (CNT) and graphene oxide (GO) — are also known to inhibit oxidants and eliminate free radicals. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a special kind of carbon material with high chemical stability, small particle size, and excellent dispersion properties. They are also easily functionalized and have garnered significant attention in scientific research and for practical industry applications.

Hindered amines (HA) are an important class of radical scavengers, achieving excellent anti-oxidation performance through free radical capture and hydroperoxide decomposition; they are also recyclable [12]. HA achieve the scavenging and regeneration of free radicals through the Denisov cycle [13]. The regeneration mechanism remains unclear, but the commonly accepted scheme involves nitroxide being generated when the HA react with singlet oxygen or peroxide [14]. The nitroxide quickly accepts a carbon-centered or H radical, forming an alkoxyamine, which somehow “regenerates” the nitroxide [9]. The free radicals are thereby eliminated, and the membrane exhibits excellent chemical stability. Compared to inorganic oxides and metal-ion radical scavengers, HA are more compatible with the polymer matrix and do not affect proton conduction sites, so composite membranes prepared with HA can maintain high proton conduction [15,16]. However, HA have a small molecular size and do not form chemical bonds with PFSA, so loss of HA during the operation of a composite membrane is a serious problem. One solution is to graft the HA onto large CQDs, which remain fixed in the composite membrane for two reasons: first, they form physical cross-linking sites with PFSA chains through hydrogen bonds; second, the CQDs are significantly larger than phosphoric acid, making it difficult for them to move between PFSA crystals.

In this paper, we used citric acid as the carbon source to prepare CQDs via a hydrothermal reaction. HA were then used to functionalize the surface of the prepared CQDs, making them radical scavengers. The prepared HA-CQDs were further processed by oxidation to reduce their basicity and make them Denisov cycle intermediates. A novel composite membrane containing HA-CQDs was then prepared and characterized. The antioxidant stability, surface morphology, side-chain functional groups, and phase separation structure of the initial and oxidation-treated HA-CQDs/PFSA composite membranes were explored in detail. We further evaluated the proton conductivity and single-cell performance of MEAs based on these composite membranes after oxidation treatment. Finally, we evaluated the free radical scavenging ability of the composite membrane using a DPPH free radical scavenging assay.

4-amino-2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-piperidin (97%, HA), citric acid (98%), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 36.5%), ferrous ammonium sulfate (NH4FeSO4.7H2O),1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. Aquivion® dispersion (25%) with 790 g/eq equivalent weight was obtained from Solvay Specialty Polymers. Gore 740 membrane (18 μm, GORE-SELECT® membrane, USA) was used as the benchmark.

Citric acid was dissolved in deionized water and mixed at uniform speed for 2 h to obtain a precursor solution. The hydrothermal reaction was carried out in a tetrafluoroethylene-lined hydrothermal reactor with 20 mL of precursor solution. The reaction lasted for 6 h at 200 °C, then the materials were allowed to cool naturally to room temperature to obtain the reaction solution. Unmodified CQDs were obtained by removing the supernatant using centrifugation (10,000 r/min). The CQD yield was about 60%.

1.0 g of 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidinamine and 0.02 g of dimethylaminopyridine (DMAP) were added to 2.0 g of unmodified CQD solution, then mixed at room temperature until uniform. The mixture was placed in a hydrothermal reactor and heated to 180 °C, then the functionalization reaction was carried out for 12 h. The solution containing functionalized CQDs was filtered with a 0.25 μm filter membrane, and the filtrate was purified for 24 h using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 1000. The HA-functionalized carbon quantum dots (HA-CQDs) were obtained by rotary evaporation. About 70% of the HA were grafted onto the surface of CQDs. Excess 30% H2O2 and phosphotungstic acid were then used to oxidize the HA-CQDs to reduce their alkalinity. The preparation method is shown in Fig. 1 .

PFSA, CQDs/PFSA, and HA-CQDs/PFSA composite membranes were prepared as follows: the CQDs or HA-CQDs nanoparticles and 25% PFSA dispersions (0.3:100, wt%) were dispersed in a mixed solution of H2O and isopropanol to form a polymer dispersion (10 wt%). A layer of polymer dispersion (150 μm wet membrane) was cast with a base film (PI membrane). An ePTFE microporous membrane was laid onto this polymer film, and another layer of polymer dispersion (150 μm wet membrane) was cast on top of the ePTFE microporous membrane. The membrane was dried at 60 °C for 10 min to remove the solvent, then it was thermally annealed at 180 °C for 60 min. The thickness of the obtained membranes was about 15 μm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Properties of the HA-CQDs and HA-CQDs/PFSA composite membranes
ce:section.ce:para:
A Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, PerkinElmer, Frontier) was used to characterize the chemical structure of the prepared CQDs, HA-CQDs, and the HA-CQDs/PFSA composite membranes. The effective diameter and polydispersity of the CQDs and HA-CQDs nanoparticles in a water–ethanol solution were characterized using dynamic light scattering (DLS) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). After setting the solution parameters (ethanol, viscosity = 1.084 cp, ref. index fluid = 1.357) and laser parameters (wavelength = 659.0 nm, angle = 90.00), 6 mL of the solution (6 mg of particles dispersed in 6 mL of ethanol) was placed in a 10 mL quartz cuvette, and the lognormal size distribution (including effective diameter, half width, and polydispersity) and baseline index were measured using a wide-angle dynamic light-scattering analyzer (Brookhaven BI-200SM, USA); each sample solution was tested three times at 25 °C. Particle dispersion in the solution was characterized using a zeta potential analyzer. The titration method was used to measure the ion exchange capacity (IEC) values of all the membranes: the membranes (0.5–1.0 g) were added into 20 mL of 0.5 mol L–1 KOH solution and left to react for 24 h. The solution was then titrated using 0.5 mol L–1 HCl solution and phenolphthalein as the indicator. The IEC values of the membranes were obtained from the difference between the volume of the sample membrane (HCl solution) and the control group (no membrane added into the KOH solution). The microphase separation structures of the various membrane were analyzed by small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS), carried out using a Rigaku D/Max 2500 v/Pc (Smart Lab, 9 kW), and the scattering vector q was obtained from Eq. (2.1):
(2.1)
q =
4 π
sin
θ
λ

The water uptake and swelling ratio were tested and obtained from the size and weight differences between a dry membrane (heated at 80 °C for 2 h) and a wet membrane (immersed in water for 24 h). The detailed experimental conditions are provided in our previous paper [17]. The membranes’ water uptake and swelling ratio were calculated from Eqs. (2.2) and (2.3):
(2.2)
W a t e r
u p t a k e ( % ) = 100 ×
W
w e t
-
W
d r y
W
d r y
(2.3)
S w e l l i n g r a t i o
( % )
= 100 ×
L
w e t
−
L
d r y
L
d r y

The through-plane conductivity of various membranes was tested and analyzed with a Membrane Test System (MTS740, Scribner). Ambient temperature and a humidity controller were used, and all membranes were preconditioned for 2 h. The high-frequency intercept (Rhf) was fitted with a Voigt equivalent circuit model using selected impedance data. The through-plane conductivity (S cm–1) is the quotient of the dry membrane thickness L (cm), effective area A (cm2), and Rhf (Ω), as indicated in Eq. (2.4):
(2.4)
σ =
L
R
h f
∗ A

A fuel cell performance measurement system (850e) was used to analyze and evaluate the single-cell performance and EIS of membrane electrode assemblies (MEA) based on all the membranes under 50% RH at 80 °C. The MEAs were assembled as follows [18]. Catalyst-coated membranes (CCMs) were prepared by a direct thermal transfer method, using a thermal transfer temperature of 180 °C and a pressure of 0.5 MPa. The anode and cathode catalyst layers contained 47.6 wt% commercial Pt/C and Solvay ionomer (EW = 790). The Pt loading was 0.28 mg cm–2 for the cathode CL and 0.08 mg cm–2 for the anode CL. MEAs were then prepared using two sheets of gas diffusion layers and CCMs. The active area of each MEA was 50 cm2.

The MEAs’ fuel cell performance was evaluated using the same fuel cell performance measurement system. The stoichiometries of the feed gases were 2.0 for air and 1.3 for hydrogen. The current density was scanned from 0 to 2 A cm2 to obtain polarization curves.

The activity of the antioxidants was tested using a DPPH· assay. This method is described by the Brand-Williams mechanism, and the process was carried out as previously reported [19]. Specifically, the DPPH· was dissolved in ethanol in a controlled concentration of 0.2 mmol L–1. A membrane containing antioxidants was immersed in 20 mL of DPPH· solution. The DPPH· reacted with the antioxidants, then the solution's absorbance was analyzed using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan), monitoring the absorbance at 518 nm and recording it at 5-min intervals. The scavenging capacity of the composite membrane was calculated using Eq. (2.5):
(2.5)
E =
A 0
−
A t
A 0
∗ 100 %
where A0 is the absorbance of the 0.02 mol L–1 DPPH· solution and At is the absorbance of the DPPH· solution in which the proton membrane was immersed for various amounts of time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of CQDs and HA-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Novel CQDs were prepared by a hydrothermal reaction using citric acid (CA) as the carbon source. To improve its free radical scavenging ability, HA was used to functionalize the carbon dots’ surface during the hydrothermal reaction, with methanesulfonic acid as the dehydrating agent and DMAP as the catalyst. Fig. 1 presents the synthesis process for CQDs and HA-CQDs.

The chemical structures of the CQDs and HA-CQDs were characterized using FTIR. Comparison of the results for citric acid, CQDs, HA, and HA-CQDs (Fig. S1(a)) shows that the peak at 1734 cm–1 was present for CQDs and HA-CQDs, indicating C=C bonds were generated and the synthesized CQDs contained some unsaturated bonds; the peaks at 1630 cm–1 and 1389 cm–1 are assigned to vibrations of the -COO- bond, indicating that abundant oxygen-containing groups were present on the surfaces of the citric acid, CQDs, and HA-CQDs. The peak at 1584 cm–1 corresponds to the deformation vibration of the secondary amide N-H, and the stretching vibration of the secondary amide C-N bond is present at 1230 cm–1. The peak at 1120 cm–1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of N-OH, also indicating that the HA were grafted onto the CQDs surfaces.

Fig. S1 (b-g) presents the 1H and 13C NMR spectra of CA, CQDs, and HA-CQDs. When comparing the 1H NMR spectra of CA and the CQDs, the chemical shift at 5.57 ppm is assigned to -CH=CH-, indicating the presence of unsaturated bonds. Comparison of the 13C NMR spectra shows chemical shifts in the CQDs at 127 ppm and 130 ppm, indicating the formation of unsaturated bonds. In addition, the chemical shift at 180 ppm is assigned to ester groups, demonstrating these were synthesized.

In comparing the 1H NMR spectra of CQDs and HA-CQDs, the chemical shift at 6.7 ppm is assigned to -NH-; this peak appeared in the HA-CQDs, indicating the presence of an amide bond. The chemical shift at 1–2 ppm in the HA-CQDs spectrum is assigned to -CH3 and -CH2-, revealing that HA was grafted onto the surface of the CQDs, and HA-CQDs therefore were successfully prepared. The 13C NMR spectra of CQDs and HA-CQDs reinforced this conclusion: a new chemical shift appeared at 170 ppm, showing amide bonds were produced. The chemical shifts at 13.92, 32.60, 37.35, 39.85, and 70.90 ppm also demonstrated that the HA-CQDs were synthesized by the reaction of CQDs and HA. The FTIR and NMR results demonstrate that HA reacted with the oxygen-containing groups on the CQDs’ surface, confirming that HA-CQDs were synthesized.

The particle size, distribution, and dispersion stability of the prepared HA-CQDs were characterized by laser DLS, zeta potential analysis, and TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 (a) and (b), the DLS results indicated that the prepared CQDs and HA-CQDs had a relatively uniform distribution (the polydispersity was 0.176 and 0.209, respectively), and their effective diameters were 5.67 and 7.06 nm, respectively. TEM imaging of the HA-CQDs particles also showed the prepared CQDs were uniformly distributed, with a particle size range of 3–8 nm. Zeta potential was used to evaluate the dispersion performance of CQDs and HA-CQDs in a water–ethanol mixed solution. Fig. 2 (c) and (d) show the CQDs and HA-CQDs had high zeta potentials because their surfaces had abundant -COOH and -NH2 groups, which possess the same electric charge, so the repulsion between the nanoparticles kept them stably dispersed in the solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance and characterization of HA-CQDs/PFSA composite membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The Gore 740, PFSA, CQDs/PFSA, and HA-CQDs/PFSA composite membranes’ water uptake, swelling ratio, and IEC were measured, and the results are presented in Table 1 . The Gore 740 had the lowest IEC value because the membrane was composed of long side-chain PFSA ionomers and a small number of inorganic nanoparticles without proton-conducting groups. Compared with the PFSA membrane, the IEC of the CQDs/PFSA and HA-CQDs/PFSA composite membranes was only slightly lower, due to the abundant -COOH groups on the CQDs. The IEC, water uptake, and swelling ratio of the PFSA, CQDs/PFSA, and HA-CQDs/PFSA composite membranes were similar, guaranteeing their proton conduction and dimensional stability would meet the requirements for fuel cell operation.

The mechanical properties of PFSA, CQDs/PFSA, and HA-CQDs/PFSA were evaluated using the stress-strain curve presented in Fig. S6. The PFSA membrane had a tensile strength of 41.68 MPa and an elongation at break of 176%. Incorporating HA-CQDs notably enhanced the tensile strength to 46.2 MPa, reduced its elongation at break to 143%, suggesting that the HA-CQDs toughened the PFSA. On the one hand, the nanoparticle structure of the HA-CQDs concentrated the stress during the stretching process, thereby improving the composite membrane's mechanical strength. On the other hand, the composite membrane's mechanical properties were also improved by -N- or -NH- groups forming intermolecular hydrogen bonds with sulfonic acid groups.

The proton conductivity of the PFSA membrane and the HA-CQDs/PFSA composite membranes with various doping dosages under different RHs are presented in Fig. 3 . The results indicate that the proton conductivity of the HA-CQDs/PFSA membrane dropped as the doping dosage increased under high RH. But the proton conductivity of the membrane increased slightly when a small amount of HA-CQDs was added, because the large number of hydrophilic groups in the HA-CQDs increased the composite membrane's water retention capacity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of chemical stability for PEM
ce:section.ce:para: The chemical stability of the composite membranes was evaluated using a common ex situ accelerated measurement method that involved treating the membranes with Fenton's reagent. In the Fenton reaction, free radicals are generated using hydrogen peroxide and Fe2+ under heat [20]. The composite membranes immersed in Fenton's reagent were attacked by -OH• and -HOO• free radicals. This experiment simulated the process of hydrogen peroxide being produced through the secondary electrochemical reaction of H2 and O2 under operating conditions, whereby the polymer matrix in a PEM is destroyed by free radicals generated by the decomposition of H2O2 [21]. Fig. 4 (a) shows the mass loss of various membranes when they were immersed in Fenton's reagent for 1, 3, and 5 days. The PFSA membrane lost 1.32 wt% per day and a total of 6.76 wt% after 5 days. In comparison, the Gore 740 and HA-CQDs/PFSA membranes exhibited excellent stability in Fenton's solution; the Gore 740 lost only 0.32 wt% per day and 1.6 wt% after 5 days, and the HA-CQDs/PFSA lost 0.36 wt% per day and 1.8 wt% after 5 days. These results show that both membranes achieved excellent chemical stability by decomposing hydrogen peroxide or scavenging free radicals. It is worth noting that the rate of mass loss in the HA-CQDs/PFSA membrane was constant for the same treatment time, indicating that the HA-CQDs were not consumed but instead were as cyclically regenerable as Gore 740 membrane (which had a similar mass loss rate per unit of time). Fig. 4(b) shows the mass loss of the HA-CQDs/PFSA composite membrane with various HA-CQDs doping dosages, treated with Fenton's reagent for different amounts of time. The results show that a small amount of HA-CQDs were able to achieve excellent chemical stability, and as more HA-CQDs were added, the composite membrane had better chemical stability, up to a point.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of chemical degradation on structure and morphology
ce:section.ce:para: To systematically study the effects of free radical degradation on the morphology, nanostructure, and chemical structure of the PEM, we analyzed the surface morphology, phase separation structure, and side-chain functional groups of the initial and treated membranes [21].

Surface morphology has an important impact on the physicochemical and electrochemical properties of PEMs, and it can also affect degradation [22]. SEM images of the various composite membrane surfaces before and after treatment with Fenton's reagent for 1, 3, and 5 days are presented in Figs. S2–S5. Figs. S2 and S3 show that the surfaces of the PFSA and CQDs/PFSA membranes presented a rough appearance as well as some defects and cracks after 1 day of immersion in Fenton's reagent, with more serious defects and cracks appearing as the immersion time increased, implying that the surface of these membranes was destroyed by free radicals. In comparison, the surfaces of the Gore 740 and HA-CQDs/PFSA composite membranes retained their smoothness, and no obvious defects or cracks had appeared even after 5 days of immersion. These results confirmed that HA-CQDs were effectively scavenging free radicals generated by Fenton's reagent, ensuring the membranes remained protected from free radical attack and rapid destruction.

When the membranes were treated with Fenton's reagent, their chemical structures were attacked by free radicals, and the number of side-chain functional groups correspondingly decreased [23]. FTIR was used to characterize changes in the chemical structures of the various PEMs, which were immersed in Fenton's reagent for 0, 1, 3, or 5 days. As shown in Fig. 5 (a–d), the peak around 1423 cm–1 is assignable to C-S bonds, the peak around 1070 cm–1 is assignable to S-OH groups, and the peak around 969 cm–1 is assignable to C-F bonds [24]. It was observed that the intensity ratios (S-OH to C-F) of the PFSA and CQDs/PFSA membranes at 1070 cm–1 and 969 cm–1 were significantly reduced when the membranes were immersed in Fenton's reagent, indicating that part of the -SO3H groups within the membrane had been degraded and washed away. However, the intensity ratios of the HA-CQDs/PFSA and Gore 740 membranes declined only slightly, demonstrating that fewer -SO3H groups were degraded under identical treatment conditions. The IEC values of the membranes treated with Fenton's reagent for different times further supported this conclusion: compared with raw PFSA and CQDs/PFSA membranes, the IEC value of the HA-CQDs/PFSA membrane dropped only slightly after treatment for 5 days. It is well known that -SO3H groups work as proton transfer carriers in PFSA-based PEMs; hence, the proton conductivity and cell performance of membranes drop when the number of -SO3H groups decreases. These results show that the introduction of HA free radical scavengers protected the chemical structure of the PFSA from free radical damage, and the HA-CQDs/PFSA composite membrane thus exhibited high chemical stability.

The microphase separation structure was generated by the isolation and localization of hydrophilic and hydrophobic groups. Therefore, destruction of the side-chain proton-conducting groups and damage to the membrane surface morphology inevitably led to disruption of the phase separation structure in the damaged composite membranes, reducing the continuity between hydrophilic regions and proton transport channels [22]. We characterized the phase separation structure of the PEM using SAXS. Fig. 6 (a) and (b) compare the SAXS profiles of the various membranes before and after oxidation treatment with Fenton's reagent. The initial scattering profiles of the various membranes are presented in Fig. 6(a). The PFSA membrane had the smallest spaces between water clusters, suggesting that the raw PFSA membrane possessed an excellent phase separation structure, which was subsequently impacted by CQDs, HA-CQDs, and metal oxide radical scavengers. The Gore 740 had large distances between hydrophilic regions, implying that its proton transport channels deteriorated when the large metal oxide radical scavengers were introduced into the PFSA matrix. When immersed in Fenton's reagent for 5 days, the PFSA and CQDs/PFSA membranes exhibited broad peaks and a large rightward shift in the ionomer peak; these changes indicated the hydrophilic zone of the membrane was partially destroyed, blurring the interface between the hydrophilic and hydrophobic zones. In contrast, the ion peak positions of the Gore 740 and HA-CQDs/PFSA composite membranes showed negligible changes under identical conditions, suggesting their phase separation structures were maintained. Therefore, the nanostructures and proton transport channels of the membranes were protected from free radicals in the presence of HA-CQDs, which acted as free radical scavengers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of chemical degradation on PEM
ce:section.ce:para: To better understand the degree of degradation, especially how the membrane's performance was affected by chemical degradation [23], we compared the proton conductivity and polarization curves of cells based on different PEMs subjected to different treatment times. Fig. 7 shows the change in through-plane proton conductivity with membranes immersed in Fenton's reagent for various times under 50% RH condition. The proton conductivity of the PFSA and CQDs/PFSA composite membranes rapidly decreased as the treatment time increased, dropping sharply from 36.5 to 13.6 mS cm–1 for the PFSA membrane and from 34.2 to 14.8 mS cm–1 for the CQDs/PFSA membrane upon immersion in Fenton's reagent for 5 days.

The reasons for these results are that the proton conductivity of the PFSA PEM is directly associated with the sulfonic acid ion clusters, so when part of the side-chain functional groups or the main chain is destroyed by free radicals, the proton conductivity of the membrane lessens. However, the Gore 740 and HA-CQDs composite membranes retained their high proton conductivity after immersion in Fenton's reagent for the same amount of time, the Gore 740 membrane dropping by only 13.5% and the HA-CQDs by only 17.2%. These results implied that the prepared HA-CQDs/PFSA composite membrane possessed excellent ability to inhibit free radicals, and that the HA-CQDs could scavenge free radicals almost as well as the Gore 740 membrane, which contains a free radical scavenger. These results are consistent with the composite membranes' chemical stability.

The significant changes in the chemical structure, surface morphology, and proton conductivity of the PEMs undoubtedly had a critical impact on the cell performance of MEAs based on these membranes [25]. Membranes treated with Fenton's reagent for 0, 4, 8, 12, and 16 h were used to prepare corresponding MEAs whose single-cell performance was tested at 80 °C and 50% RH. As shown in Fig. 8 (a–d), all the MEAs had excellent cell performance and high OCV initially, meaning that the introduced CQDs and HA-CQDs did not lower the PEM's cell performance. Generally speaking, the introduction of inorganic nano-radical scavengers (such as CeO2, MnO2, and ZrO2) will cause the proton conductivity and cell performance to significantly decrease; the cell voltage at 1 A cm–2 of an MEA based on Gore 740 membrane is 18 mV lower than that of raw PFSA membrane and 12 mV lower than that of a HA-CQDs/PFSA composite membrane. This is because the particles are large, have an incompatible interface, and have no proton-conducting groups on their surface. However, the cell performance and OCV of the PFSA membrane were noticeably reduced when the membrane was treated with Fenton's reagent: the OCV dropped from 0.958 to 0.896 V, and the cell voltage at 1 A cm–2 went from 0.692 to 0.566 V after 8 h of immersion. Hence, the MEA could not operate normally because serious gas penetration had occurred. A similar situation transpired in the CQDs/PFSA membrane; the cell voltage at 1 A cm–2 dropped from 0.689 to 0.634 V after 8 h of immersion and decreased further to 0.582 V after 12 h of treatment. The CQDs/PFSA composite membrane experienced slightly less cell performance degradation than the PFSA membrane, because the CQDs had some unsaturated groups (such as -OH and C=C), which reacted with active free radicals and thereby reduced their concentration in the solution.

Gore 740 membrane is known to be an excellent commercial membrane with strong chemical stability and a long lifetime. After 16 h of treatment with Fenton's reagent, the MEA based on treated Gore 740 still had a high OCV and excellent cell performance: the OCV remained 0.936 V and the cell voltage was 0.642 V at 1 A cm–2. Comparatively, the HA-CQDs/PFSA composite membrane also retained a high OCV (0.927 V) and cell voltage (0.646 V), implying it had excellent chemical stability and effectively removed free radicals, ensuring the surface morphology remained intact and the proton-conducting functional groups were not attacked. Hence, the prepared HA-CQDs/PFSA composite membrane showed excellent chemical stability and should have a long lifetime under operating conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Free radical scavenging ability of composite membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Many methods have been reported for evaluating the free radical scavenging ability of an antioxidant molecule, but the DPPH· scavenging assay is one of most widely used and easiest methods to assess the activity of antioxidants. 1,1-diphenyl-2-trinitrophenylhydrazine (DPPH·) is a stable one-electron radical centered on a nitrogen atom and can scavenge free radicals in a strictly 1:1 ratio [26]. DPPH· appears dark purple in ethanol solution, and in a UV–vis spectrophotometer, the maximum absorption peak appears at 518 nm (see Fig. 9 (a)). In the presence of free radical scavengers, it can accept a hydrogen atom to generate DPPH2; as this occurs, the solution gradually changes from dark purple to light yellow, and the absorption peak at 518 nm decreases. The color change is related to the concentration of DPPH· and the number of free radicals it accepts. DPPH· content is analyzed by measuring the solution's absorbance [27].

Fig. 9 (b) and (c) show the solution's absorbance and the free radical scavenging rate of various membranes versus immersion time when the DPPH· concentration was 0.2 mmol L–1. The absorbance of the DPPH· solution in which the PFSA membrane was immersed for 240 min decreased only slightly, from 0.796 to 0.744. This result indicated that the PFSA membrane had difficulty eliminating free radicals because it had no free radical scavengers. Fig. 9(c) shows that only about 6.6% of the DPPH free radicals were eliminated, by reacting with defects and sulfonic acid groups in the PFSA polymer; hence, the side chains of the PFSA polymers tended to decompose, and the membrane was readily destroyed in the presence of such a high free radical concentration. Since the CQDs possessed some hydroxyl groups and unsaturated bonds, the absorbance of the DPPH· solution decreased to 0.686, indicating a 17.2% free radical scavenging rate as some of the DPPH· were eliminated by electron transfer to these groups. When the HA-CQDs composite membrane was immersed in the DPPH· solution, its color gradually changed from dark purple to light yellow, and its absorbance at 518 nm also clearly decreased. The same occurred with the Gore 740 membrane, although the decline in its absorbance was slightly slower. The absorbance of the DPPH· solutions in which the Gore 740 and HA-CQDs/PFSA membranes were immersed for 240 min dropped sharply to 0.354 and 0.319, decreases of 55.5% and 59.6%, respectively. These results suggest that the Gore 740 and HA-CQDs/PFSA composite membranes contained effective free radical scavengers, and DPPH free radicals were effectively eliminated in a short time. Fig. 9 (c) also shows that the HA-CQDs/PFSA and Gore 740 membranes yielded free radical scavenging rates of 82.3% and 76.6% (the absorbance of DPPH2 at 518 nm was 0.172). Commercial Gore 740 membrane contains transition-state metal oxide radical scavengers, whose large size and complicated elimination mechanism (which requires H+) resulted in a slightly lower free radical scavenging rate than that of the prepared HA-CQDs composite membrane; the latter had smaller particles and better dispersion, and the H atoms from amino groups were easily accepted by DPPH·. In addition, the electrons of DPPH· could be transferred to the unsaturated groups in HA-CQDs. The HA-CQDs could therefore terminate DPPH radicals not only by donating H atoms but also by facilitating electron transfer. These experimental results explain why the HA-CQDS/PFSA composite membrane could readily scavenge free radicals and had excellent chemical stability.

In this work, carbon quantum dots were synthesized via a hydrothermal reaction, then functionalized with hindered amine radical scavengers. Proton exchange membranes with effective free radical scavenging ability and excellent chemical stability were prepared by introducing synthesized HA-CQDs into the MEAs. When treated with Fenton's reagent, the prepared HA-CQDs/PFSA composite membrane experienced low mass loss and excellent free radical scavenging ability. Analysis using SEM, FTIR, and SAXS revealed that after oxidation treatment, the HA-CQDs/PFSA composite membrane retained a completely defect-free surface morphology, as well as intact side-chain functional groups and phase separation structure. The antioxidant performance of the HA-CQDs/PFSA was close to that of commercial Gore 740 membrane. The proton conductivity and cell performance results suggest that the HA-CQDs/PFSA composite membrane treated with free radicals retained excellent proton conductivity and superior cell performance. When the scavenging ability of the composite membrane was evaluated using DPPH radicals, the results proved that the prepared HA-CQDs could effectively scavenge free radicals, mitigating the chemical degradation of the PFSA PEM by free radicals generated during the electrochemical reaction and thereby improving the PEM's chemical stability.

This manuscript under supervision by Jiantao Fan and Hui Li, Henghui Huang performed sample preparation and data analysis. Shaoyi Xu developed chemical stability mechanics analysis. Feiyan Luo and Jiexin Zou performed sample test and structure fabrication. All authors have read and contributed to the manuscript. And all authors agree the final version to be published.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2020YFB1505500 and 2018YFB1502503), the Guangdong Key R&D Program (2020B0909040001), the Shenzhen Science, Technology Projects for Sustainable Development (KCXFZ202002011010317), and Shenzhen Free Exploration (JCYJ20190809163611271).
